coredata.dc:title: Photoelectrochemical aptasensor for sensitive detection of tetracycline in soil based on CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction: Improved photoactivity enabled by Z-scheme electron transfer pathway
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhijie
Jiang, Kaituo
Jiang, Huihui
Zhang, Hang
Liu, Qian
You, Tianyan
coredata.dc:description: Exploring effective methods for tetracycline (TC) detection in soil has great significance because of its emerging environmental problem and increasing threat to soil quality and general public health worldwide. In this work, a sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor toward TC detection was designed and constructed based on an efficient photosensitive material of Z-scheme CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction. Due to the sensitization of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) on the BiOBr nanoflowers, the photocurrent intensity of the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was enhanced about 5.0-fold and 8.0-fold than that of pure BiOBr and CdTe under visible-light irradiation, which was attributed to the low electron-hole combination efficiency, high visible light utilization efficiency, and high carrier density of the heterojunction. On the merits of the excellent PEC activity of the CdTe-BiOBr and the specificity of the aptamer, the proposed PEC aptasensor has the advantages of satisfying linear range (from 10 to 1500 pM), low detection limit (9.25 pM), good selectivity, and reproducibility. In addition, acceptable accuracy was obtained for TC detection in real soil sample, giving acceptable accuracy in comparison with the referenced high-performance liquid chromatography-diode array detector method, revealing a promising avenue for accurate and ultrasensitive estimation of other kinds of contaminants in the broad field of analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Z-scheme heterojunction
BiOBr nanoflower
CdTe quantum dot
Photoelectrochemical aptasensor
Tetracycline detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PEC performance of CdTe-BiOBr and its PEC signal enhancement exploration
Characterization of the fabricated PEC aptasensor
Performance of the PEC aptasensor for TC detection
Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the PEC aptasensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A, B) and HRTEM (C) images of BiOBr; TEM (D, E) and HRTEM (F) images of CdTe-BiOBr; SEM (G) image of BiOBr; XRD (H) patterns of BiOBr and CdTe-BiOBr; XPS (I) survey scan of CdTe-BiOBr.

(A) Photocurrent responses of different modified ITO electrodes (CdTe, BiOBr and CdTe-BiOBr) recorded at 0 V bias potential in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4); (B) Schematic diagram of electron transfer mechanism for Z-scheme CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction.
(A) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (B) Mott-Schottky plots of BiOBr and CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction.

(A) PEC responses and (B) Nyquist plots (EIS) of ITO (a), CdTe-BiOBr/ITO (b), aptamer/CdTe-BiOBr/ITO (c) and TC/aptamer/CdTe-BiOBr/ITO (d). The PEC test was performed at 0 V bias potential in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4). EIS measurements were studied in 0.1 M KCl with 5.0 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4-.

Effects of experimental conditions on the photocurrent response of aptamer/CdTe-BiOBr/ITO aptasensor: (A) applied potential; (B) aptamer concentration; (C) binding time of TC with aptamer and (D) solution pH.

(A) PEC responses of the fabricated aptasensor toward different concentrations of TC and (B) the calibration curve (n = 3) for TC detection; (C) selectivity and (D) reproducibility of the developed PEC aptasensor.
Schematic diagram for the fabrication and detection processes of the PEC aptasensor toward TC detection.
Results for TC detection in soil sample with the developed PEC aptasensor.

head.ce:title: Photoelectrochemical aptasensor for sensitive detection of tetracycline in soil based on CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction: Improved photoactivity enabled by Z-scheme electron transfer pathway

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Modern Agriculture Equipment and Technology, School of Agricultural Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring effective methods for tetracycline (TC) detection in soil has great significance because of its emerging environmental problem and increasing threat to soil quality and general public health worldwide. In this work, a sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor toward TC detection was designed and constructed based on an efficient photosensitive material of Z-scheme CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction. Due to the sensitization of CdTe quantum dots (QDs) on the BiOBr nanoflowers, the photocurrent intensity of the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was enhanced about 5.0-fold and 8.0-fold than that of pure BiOBr and CdTe under visible-light irradiation, which was attributed to the low electron-hole combination efficiency, high visible light utilization efficiency, and high carrier density of the heterojunction. On the merits of the excellent PEC activity of the CdTe-BiOBr and the specificity of the aptamer, the proposed PEC aptasensor has the advantages of satisfying linear range (from 10 to 1500 pM), low detection limit (9.25 pM), good selectivity, and reproducibility. In addition, acceptable accuracy was obtained for TC detection in real soil sample, giving acceptable accuracy in comparison with the referenced high-performance liquid chromatography-diode array detector method, revealing a promising avenue for accurate and ultrasensitive estimation of other kinds of contaminants in the broad field of analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic contamination in soil, which mainly originated from animal manure as fertilizer and reclaimed wastewater for irrigation, has aroused much concern in recent years due to its damage to ecological environment and human health (Liu et al., 2017a; Xiang et al., 2016). Early in 2001, the Food and Drug Administration has set an ecotoxic effect trigger value of 100 mg/kg for the antibiotic residue in soil (FDA, 2001). Among various kinds of antibiotics residue, tetracycline (TC) is one of the most dominant antibiotics observed in soil (Lyu et al., 2020). At present, traditional techniques with the characteristic of accuracy have been used for TC detection, including high-performance liquid chromatography-fluorescence (HPLC-FL) (Du et al., 2015b), high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) (Wang et al., 2019a), and capillary electrophoresis (Mu et al., 2012), but practical application is limited to some extent by the expensive equipment and specialized laboratory operation of these methods above (Yang et al., 2018). Thus, it’s extremely important to establish simple and low-cost methods to detect TC in soil, which is essential for protecting the ecological environment and human health.

Due to the advantages of photoelectrochemical (PEC) bioanalysis and unique properties of aptamers, PEC aptasensor has emerged as a prospective method and offered a new exciting technique for detecting different types of environmental pollutants, such as organic compounds, inorganic ions, and so on (Deng et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019b). Aptamer and PEC active material are two relatively independent parts in the PEC aptasensor, the aptamer is related to the selectivity of the sensor, while the detection sensitivity is more intimately associated with the PEC active materials, which can make the detection performance of the PEC aptasensor more excellent (Fu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019a) As a new photoactive material with a characteristic layered crystal structure and high efficiency of visible light utilization (Zhang et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2016), bismuth oxobromide (BiOBr) has attracted much attention in photocatalytic energy conversion and environmental remediation. However, the application of pure BiOBr material is limited by its rapid recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pair, and the formation of heterojunction by combining BiOBr with other nanomaterials is regarded as an efficient protocol to solve this problem (Gao et al., 2019; Cui et al., 2014). For example, Li’s group put forward a Type-II heterojunction of carbon nitride/BiOBr, the PEC investigations indicated that the addition of carbon nitride in BiOBr can reduce the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs effectively, leading to enhancing the PEC response of BiOBr and thus improving the detection performance of the fabricated PEC sensor (Yan et al., 2018a). Compared with Type-II heterojunction, the formation of Z-scheme heterojunction is greatly desirable due to the improved light-harvesting capacity, spatially separated reductive and oxidative active sites, and well preserved strong redox capacity (Xu et al., 2018). In a recent report, BiOBr has been coupled with Bi2MoO6 to form a Z-scheme heterojunction, the photocurrent of BiOBr-Bi2MoO6 was significant enhanced than pure BiOBr and Bi2MoO6 due to the higher separation efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pair in BiOBr-Bi2MoO6 (Wang et al., 2017). Such amplified photocurrent signal, which is generated from the formation of BiOBr based Z-scheme heterojunction, is beneficial for facilitating the analytical performance of the PEC sensor, however, investigation of BiOBr based Z-scheme heterojunction in the fabrication of PEC sensor has not been reported yet to our best knowledge.

In this protocol, a Z-scheme heterojunction of CdTe-BiOBr was designed and synthesized to construct a sensitive PEC aptasensor for TC detection. Firstly, the heterojunction of CdTe-BiOBr was synthesized by a hydrothermal method, and the crystal structure, morphology as well as the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers of the resultant heterojunction was characterized. Then, a PEC aptasensor was fabricated by employing CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction as photosensitive material and aptamer as recognition elements, which could guarantee the sensitivity and selectivity of the PEC aptasensor. Furthermore, the calibration equation was constructed by the relationship of photocurrent variation of the PEC aptasensor and TC concentration, and the analytical applicability of the aptasensor toward TC detection in soil sample was also investigated.
All materials, reagents and instruments were presented in the "Supplementary Information".

CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was synthesized by a modified hydrothermal method (Liu et al., 2020) using the synthesized BiOBr above as a precursor. And the preparation of BiOBr was described in the "Supplementary Information". In simple terms, NaHTe solution was prepared by reacting 0.046 g NaBH4 and 0.065 g Te in the deionized water. After filled with nitrogen for 15 min, the solution was kept in the 4 °C fridge 12 h. An aqueous solution containing 0.114 g CdCl2·2.5H2O and 0.090 g BiOBr was stirred for 15 min. Then, 75 μL of MPA was poured into this mixture and the pH was adjusted with NaOH solution (1 M). Subsequently, 2 mL of NaHTe solution was added to this solution and stirred for 10 min. The CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was collected after the solution refluxed at 100 °C and washed with ethanol, and dried at 60 °C. CdTe quantum dots (QDs) were prepared by the same procedure as described above without the addition of BiOBr, and the synthesis of CdTe QDs was verified by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra (Fig. S1). Based on the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CdTe QDs, the diameters of CdTe QDs (3.46 nm) and the concentration of its stock solution (33.6 μM) were calculated by the method listed in Supporting Information.

Before constructing the TC aptasensor, indium-tin oxides (ITO) slices (2.0 × 1 cm2) were cleaned with 1 M NaOH solution, and then washed ultrasonically with ethanol and ultrapure water for 15 min, respectively. Subsequently, the ITO slices were dried at room temperature for further use (Chen et al., 2020). The construction process of the PEC aptasensor was illustrated in Scheme 1. Firstly, 20 μL of 2 mg/mL CdTe-BiOBr suspension was casted onto the ITO electrode and dried in an incubator (37 °C). Next, 10 μL of 0.5% chitosan solution was coated on the CdTe-BiOBr/ITO. After drying, 20 μL of 2 μM TC aptamer solution (dispersed in 0.01 M PBS) was dropped onto the electrode surface and stored at 4 °C for 12 h. Due to the cationic character of chitosan, the aptamer with negatively charge can interact electrostatically with chitosan. After removing any unbounded aptamers with PBS (0.01 M), the aptasensor (aptamer/CdTe-BiOBr/ITO) was obtained.

For TC analysis, 20 μL different concentrations of TC solution were dropped onto the resultant aptasensor and incubated for 2.5 h at 4 °C and then rinsed slightly with PBS (0.01 M) followed by the respective PEC measurements. The applied potential of the PEC measurements was −0.2 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for TC detection.

The morphologies of the as-prepared CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction and BiOBr were characterized by TEM. A flower-like structure with a diameter of 1.2 µm was observed for CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction ( Fig. 1D), which was identical to the BiOBr nanoflowers depicted by TEM (Fig. 1A) and SEM (Fig. 1G), revealing that the synthesis process of CdTe QDs has no obvious effect on the morphology of the BiOBr nanoflowers. In addition, compared with the high-magnification TEM image of pure BiOBr (Fig. 1B), as shown in Fig. 1E, the CdTe QDs were attached on the edge of BiOBr in the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction. HRTEM was employed to further explore the structure of the BiOBr and CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction (Fig. 1C and F), which revealed interplanar spacing of 0.28 nm and 0.37 nm, corresponding to the (1 1 0) crystal planes of BiOBr and the (1 1 1) crystal planes of CdTe QDs, respectively (Du et al., 2015a). The above results indicate that CdTe QDs were successfully anchored on the BiOBr nanoflowers by the hydrothermal method.

The crystalline structures of the resultant CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction and BiOBr were studied by XRD (Fig. 1H). The characteristic diffraction peaks of CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was the same as pure BiOBr, which was corresponded to the (1 0 1), (1 1 0), (1 1 2), (1 1 3), (2 1 1), and (2 1 2) crystal planes of tetragonal BiOBr, respectively (as indexed by JCPDS card No. 78–0348) (Guo et al., 2019). No diffraction peaks consistent with CdTe QDs have been detected in the XRD pattern of CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction due to the low content of CdTe QDs (Qu et al., 2019). In addition, the characteristic diffraction peak of CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was shifted towards a higher angle compared to BiOBr, which is due to the ionic radius of Cd2+ (0.97Ǻ) is smaller than that of Bi3+ (1.03Ǻ), and the Cd2+ embedded into the BiOBr microflowers and causing distortions in the lattice (Liu et al., 2014; Li et al., 2010).

XPS analysis was used to elucidate the composition of the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction. The survey XPS spectrum of CdTe-BiOBr revealed the presence of Cd, Te, W, O, and C in the heterojunction (Fig. 1F). The high resolution XPS spectrum of Bi 4f (Fig. S2A) depicts the absorption peaks at 159.2 eV and 164.5 eV, which were attributed to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, proving the Bi element in the form of Bi3+ (Cheng et al., 2011). Fig. S2B displayed the high resolution XPS spectrum of Br, the peaks occurred at 68.2 eV and 69.2 eV were separately ascribed to Br 3d5/2 and Br 3d3/2 in CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction (Guan et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S2C, two sharp signal peaks at 529.7 eV and 532.0 eV appeared in the O 1s which were associated with the Bi-O bonds of BiOBr lattice and water molecules, respectively (Yan et al., 2018b). From Figs. S2D and 2E, it can be seen that the characteristic peaks of Te 3d (582.4 eV and 572.0 eV) and Cd 3d (404.9 eV and 411.6 eV) (Liu et al., 2020). The characterizations above proved the successful synthesis of BiOBr and CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PEC performance of CdTe-BiOBr and its PEC signal enhancement exploration
ce:section.ce:para: The PEC performance of different material modified electrodes were displayed in Fig. 2A, a weak photocurrent response of 14 nA (CdTe QDs) and 22 nA (BiOBr) was observed respectively. A relatively high photocurrent (CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction) was obtained, which was approximately 8.0 and 5.0 times than CdTe QDs and BiOBr, respectively. The results suggested the formation of CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction could increase the photocurrent intensity, which is ascribed to the sensitization effect of CdTe QDs on the BiOBr material, thus resulted in the efficient electron hole pairs separation and transfer (Hu et al., 2019).

To investigate the reasons for the enhanced PEC signal of CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction, the level structures of CdTe QDs and BiOBr were explored by electrochemical method and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum. Firstly, the energy level of CdTe QDs was estimated by electrochemical method. A redox peak of gold located at +0.5 V (vs. NHE) was observed in the cyclic voltammogram of gold electrode (curve a in Fig. S3), which was scanned in the borate buffer solution. After CdTe QDs was added, the first positive sweep (0 → +1.5 V, curve b) acquired an anodic peak appeared at 1.08 V (vs. NHE). And the cathodic peak was −0.59 V (vs. NHE), which was the first negative sweep (0 → −1.2 V, curve c). Based on the detection above and reported literature (Liu et al., 2017b), the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) level of CdTe QDs was calculated to be 1.08 and −0.59 eV (vs NHE), respectively, its bandgap (Eg) was 1.67 eV (vs NHE).

The Eg of BiOBr was calculated to be 2.73 eV through its DRS (Fig. S4 inset) and Tauc’s plot (Fig. S4). The CB and VB of BiOBr were calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2), where EVB, ECB, and Eg refer to the potential of VB, CB, and bandgap, respectively. χ (6.17 eV) represents the electronegativity value of BiOBr. As the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale, Ee approximately is 4.5 eV (vs NHE) (Ye et al., 2012).
(1)
E
VB
(
eV
)
= χ −
E
e
+ 0.5
E
g
(2)
E
CB
(
eV
)
=
E
VB
−
E
g

The CB and VB of BiOBr were calculated to be +0.31 eV and +3.04 eV, respectively (vs NHE). Based on the results above, the Z-scheme type electron transfer process of CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was addressed in Fig. 2B. Under visible light excitation, photoelectrons on the CB of BiOBr and CdTe QDs were excited to their VB respectively. The CB potential of CdTe QDs was more negative than O2/‧O2 -(−0.046 eV vs. NHE), and the VB potential of BiOBr was more positive than H2O/‧OH (2.72 eV vs. NHE) (Huang et al., 2019). Thus, a Z-scheme electron transfer pathway was constructed by the photoelectrons on the CB of BiOBr were transferred to the VB of CdTe QDs, by this means, the photoexcited electron-hole pairs in the heterojunction could separate efficiently, resulting in the amplified photocurrent of the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction (Luo et al., 2017).

Moreover, the light absorption capacity and carrier density of the materials were studied by DRS and Mott-Schottky plot. As displayed in Fig. 3A, the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction showed wider and stronger visible light absorption compared with pure BiOBr, implying higher visible light utilization of heterojunctions compared to individual materials. Furthermore, according to literature, the carrier density of electrode material was inversely proportional to the slope of the Mott-Schottky plot. Fig. 3B shows that the slope of the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was smaller than BiOBr, which indicates a high carrier density in the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction (Yang et al., 2019). From the results above, it is clear that the enhanced photocurrent signal of CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction in this work was ascribed to the following three reasons including low electron-hole combination efficiency, high visible light utilization, and high carrier density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the fabricated PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The photocurrent responses of the fabricated process of the PEC aptasensor were investigated. Fig. 4A displays that the bare ITO electrode had a small photocurrent signal (curve a). After CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was modified on the ITO, a maximal PEC response (118 nA, curve b) was observed. The photocurrent intensity decreased to 19 nA (curve c) when the aptamer was anchored owing to the steric hindrance effect of the aptamer (Yan et al., 2015). When TC was further introduced, a significant increase of photocurrent (28 nA) was recorded (curve d), which was ascribed to the fact that the TC molecule was oxidized by photogenerated holes in CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction (Shi et al., 2020). In addition, the oxidation of TC was also verified by comparison the UV–vis of TC solution before and after treatment with CdTe-BiOBr under Xe lamp irradiation. Firstly, the absorption peaks of different concentrations of TC solutions were investigated, the characteristic peak located at 356 nm was originated from TC (Wang et al., 2020), and the intensity of absorption peaks decreased with the concentration of TC decreased (Fig. S5A). The results indicated the absorption peak intensity of TC was related to its concentration, the smaller TC concentration was, the smaller absorption peak intensity. Then 1 mL TC solution (50 μM) was treated with 2 mg CdTe-BiOBr under Xe lamp irradiation for 30 min, the UV–vis spectra of TC solution before and after treatment was further studied and compared in Fig. S5B. As depicted, an obvious characteristic peak of TC located at 356 nm was observed for TC solution (50 μM), while no absorption peak was observed after treatment, which indicated TC molecule can be oxidized by CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction in this PEC system. The results indicated the PEC aptasensor has been satisfactorily constructed and can be used for the detection of TC.

EIS was a characterization method of sensing interface modification. The EIS experiment input parameters are frequency range from 0.1 to 1 × 105 Hz, a bias voltage of 0.23 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), and amplitude of 10 mV. The equivalent circuit [Rs(Cdl(RetZw))] was used to fit the Nyquist plot, in which Rs, Cdl, Ret, and Zw represents the solution resistance, double-layer capacitance, electron transfer resistance, and diffusion resistance, respectively (Liu et al., 2020). The value of semicircle diameter in the Nyquist plot represents the electron-transfer resistance (Ret) value of the modified electrode. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the bare ITO exhibited a quite small semicircle (curve a), an increase in the radius of the semicircle of the CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction was observed (curve b), indicating a successful modification step. Then, the immobilization of aptamer caused the radius of the semicircle to increase (curve c) because the aptamer molecules and negatively charged redox species of the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- mutual repulsion, that electron transport was blocked (Huang et al., 2021; Zarei et al., 2020). With the addition of TC (curve d), the configuration of the aptamer changed when bound to the target, which was beneficial to electron transfer at the electrode interface, making the radius of the semicircle decreased (Labib et al., 2012), further confirming the successful fabrication of the PEC aptasensor.

In order to obtain a better analytical performance of the PEC aptasensor, some experimental parameters which affecting the photocurrent intensity were optimized. To quantitatively compare the response of the aptasensor to TC, the photocurrent variation (∆PI) before and after incubation with TC were evaluated.

In the PEC aptasensor, the applied potential is a critical parameter since it can affect the recombination probability of the photogenerated electrons-holes. As displayed in Fig. 5A, the ∆PI increases with the applied potential from 0.1 V to −0.3 V and reaches a maximum at −0.3 V. But a relatively high voltage could damage the electrode surface (Zhang et al., 2018). Thus, −0.2 V was considered as the optimized potential applied for PEC detection in subsequent experiments. The concentration of the aptamer is an essential factor in the property of the constructed PEC aptasensor. As displayed in Fig. 5B, the ∆PI increased with the aptamer concentration from 1 µM to 2 µM. This result accorded with the fact that more TC molecules were captured due to a higher concentration of aptamer immobilized on the electrode. Nevertheless, when the aptamer concentration further increased to 5 µM, the decrease ∆PI was obtained due to the steric hindrance blockage electrons transfer (Liu et al., 2016). Therefore, 2 μM was picked as the optimum aptamer concentration and used in the following experiment. Fig. 5C illustrated the influence of the incubation time of TC in the PEC aptasensor. With the increase of the incubation time from 1 to 3 h, the ∆PI reached nearly a plateau at 2 h. To make the combination of the target and the aptamer more efficient, 2.5 h was selected as the optimal incubation time in the next experiments. Fig. 5D explains the different pH of electrolyte (pH value from 6 to 9) affecting the PEC signal of the PEC aptasensor. The experiments suggested that the photocurrent intensity of the aptasensor in 0.1 M PBS at pH 7 was bigger than at other pH values, which might be due to the fact that the structure of the aptamer binding site with the target was more beneficial to electron transfer under pH 7 (Li et al., 2017; Hianik et al., 2007). Thus, 0.1 M PBS with pH 7 was used as electrolyte in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the PEC aptasensor for TC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal experimental conditions, TC with different concentrations was detected by the constructed PEC aptasensor. Fig. 6A shows that the photocurrent gradually increased as the TC concentration increased, meaning more captured TC molecules were oxidized. And the aptasensor performed a good linear to the logarithm of TC concentration in the range of 10–1500 pM (Fig. 6B). The calibration equation (n = 3) was I = 53.4 logC +254.3 with the correlation coefficients of 0.998. The limit of detection (LOD) was 9.25 pM, which was calculated by using the probabilistic approach recommended by IUPAC (Zhu et al., 2020). In addition, compared with other methods reported in literature (Table S1), the proposed CdTe-BiOBr based PEC aptasensor showed satisfying linear range and relatively low LOD for TC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the PEC aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the fabricated PEC aptasensor was investigated by selecting several interfering substances including OTC, CTC, DOC, RM, OFL and SA. 1.5 nM TC solution, 10 nM other antibiotic solutions, 10 nM six antibiotics mixed solution (OTC, CTC, DOC, RM, OFL) and the mixed solution included 1.5 nM TC were tested under the same experimental condition. Compared with 1.5 nM TC, no distinct change in photocurrent was observed for 10 nM of other antibiotics or their mixture (Fig. 6C), proving the excellent selectivity of the constructed PEC aptasensor. To evaluate the repeatability of the aptasensor, the photocurrent of six parallel electrodes fabricated under identical conditions was obtained, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of measurements was 0.9% (Fig. 6D), demonstrating a good reproducibility of the constructed aptasensor. Fig. S6A shows the recording of the photocurrent response for the detection of 10 pM TC under10 repeated off-on-off operations, no obvious change of photocurrent was monitored, meanwhile, long-term stability was also investigated, and no significant decay of the photocurrent response was observed in 13 days (Fig. S6B), which indicated the PEC aptasensor had general stability.

To assess the analytical applicability of the constructed PEC aptasensor in actual application, standard addition method was used to analyze the TC content in soil samples, which were obtained from one household farmland of using manure fertilizer in Tianjin, China (Fig. S7). The extraction of TC was achieved by addition 1 g of soil samples to the extraction solution, the supernatant was obtained by the steps of shaking, sonicating and centrifuging according to the modified literature (Song et al., 2020). Firstly, the extracted soil samples with no TC added was detected to ensure whether TC was present in the samples. As shown in Tables 1 and S2, no TC was detected in the extracted soil samples by both the PEC aptasensor and HPLC-diode array detector (DAD) method (GB13/T 32951–2016), which might be due to TC content was lower than the limit of detection of the HPLC-DAD method (1.33 μM) and even the proposed PEC aptasensor (9.25 pM). Then, standard TC solution with different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 500 pM) was added to the extraction samples above for TC detection. The results listed in Table 1 showed that the recoveries were in the range of 96–102% with an RSD of 1.16–7.10%. In addition, the standard TC was added into the soil samples, followed by extraction of TC for detection was also investigated. 4.4 mg TC was added into 1 g of soil samples, and then extracted by the extraction solution, the recovery of the aptasensor was 73% with a RSD of 1.13%, such low recovery result was due to the extraction efficiency for TC extraction from the soil sample (Blackwell et al., 2004). To validate the accuracy of the aptasensor, the soil samples were also tested by HPLC-DAD method. Relatively consistent results were obtained while the spiked sample with a TC concentration of 30 and 120 μM was detected by the proposed aptasensor and HPLC-DAD (Table S2), it should be noted that the spiked sample used for aptasensor was diluted for detection. The results above demonstrated the developed PEC aptasensor has good feasibility and could be applied for TC detection in practical soil samples.

To sum up, a PEC aptasensor with satisfactory performance for accurate detection of TC in soil sample was proposed based on a Z-scheme CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction. By virtue of the sensitization effect of BiOBr by CdTe QDs, the CdTe-BiOBr with Z-scheme electron transfer pathway showed significantly amplified photocurrent under visible-light irradiation compared with pure CdTe QDs and BiOBr. Such enhanced PEC response was ascribed to the low electron-hole combination efficiency, high visible light utilization, and high carrier density of the designed CdTe-BiOBr heterojunction. By virtue of the sensitivity of the PEC technology and the excellent selectivity of aptasensor, the fabricated PEC aptasensor displayed good analytical performance and satisfactory recoveries for TC detection in soil samples. In view of the charming advantage of constructed PEC aptasensor, it could be used to detect various targets in the PEC detection fields.

Zhijie Guo: Investigation, Data curation, Writing − original draft. Kaituo Jiang: Investigation, Resources. Huihui Jiang: Resources, Analysis of data. Hang Zhang: Resources, Analysis of data. Qian Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing − review & editing, Funding acquisition. Tianyan You: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. PAPD-2018–87), and Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 19JDG026).

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

All experimental materials, apparatus and preparation of BiOBr nanoflower, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CdTe QDs, the calculation method of diameters and the stock solution concentration of CdTe QDs, SEM image of BiOBr, High-resolution XPS spectra of CdTe-BiOBr including Bi 4f, Br 3d, O 1s Te 3d and Cd 3d, Cyclic voltammograms of gold disc electrode recorded in a borate buffer solution in the absence and presence of CdTe QDs, Tauc’s plot to determine the band gap of BiOBr and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum of BiOBr, The assessment of the stability of the developed PEC sensor, satellite view of soil samples locations, tables for developed PEC sensor compared with different aptasensors, and HPLC for TC detection in soil.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.127498.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Table S1
Supplementary material
.
